---
title: "UrbanAnalytics"
output: pdf_document
date: "2026-02-05"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# KNOWLEDGE FILE — Urban Analytics (Website Demo)

## DESCRIPTION  
Urban Analytics is a web-based product that ranks neighborhoods using a transparent **Shift Score** to identify early signals of urban change and real-estate opportunity before they are reflected in headline prices.

The product is designed to reduce uncertainty in early-stage location decisions by translating complex spatial, environmental, and urban signals into a clear, explainable ranking.

---

## IDEA  
Build a website for real-estate investors and developers that makes neighborhood-level opportunity understandable in under one minute:  
**where to look, what is changing, and why it matters**.

Urban Analytics does not predict prices. It highlights **structural signals** that often precede changes in demand, risk, or long-term value.

---

## SUCCESSFUL OUTCOME  
A first-time user can:
1. Open the website  
2. Select a city in Italy  
3. Click on a neighborhood  

…and leave with a clear, defensible takeaway:

> “This area looks structurally interesting and worth deeper investigation.”

That conclusion must be supported by **plain-language explanations**, visible data sources, and stated limitations — not a black-box score.

---

## USERS  

### Primary users  
Real-estate investors and developers evaluating where demand, risk, or transformation may emerge next.

They are:
- non-technical
- time-constrained
- financially motivated

They want:
- fast ranking
- clear “why”
- a method page they can share internally or with partners

### Secondary users  
Analysts, advisors, or internal team members who will:
- inspect assumptions
- review data sources
- challenge methodology

The product must withstand *reasonable scrutiny*, even if it is only a demo.

---

## BUILDING A DEMO (v1)

The deliverable is a **website**, not a platform.

### Required pages
- Landing page (what it is, why it exists, who it is for)
- Demo page (interactive experience)
- Method & limitations page (credibility anchor)

### Demo experience requirements
The demo must include:
- A city map with neighborhood polygons colored by **Shift Score**
- A ranked list view of neighborhoods
- A neighborhood detail panel showing:
  - the Shift Score
  - the top contributing drivers
  - a short “Why this area?” narrative written in plain language

The demo must feel like a real product:
- clean UI
- fast interactions
- visible credibility signals (sources, assumptions, limits)

---

## NON-SUCCESS (v1)

Urban Analytics v1 explicitly does **not** aim to be:

- an automated valuation model  
- a price prediction tool  
- a real-time monitoring system  
- a causal model (“X caused prices to rise”)  

The demo does **not** require:
- paid satellite imagery
- paid mobility feeds
- user authentication
- persistent user accounts

Any heavy infrastructure (auth, background jobs, real-time ingestion, databases beyond static/API data) is out of scope unless explicitly approved.

---

## STYLE OF PRODUCT

The website should feel modern, minimal, confident, calm with mute color panel.

Design principle:
1. Show the **answer first** (map + rank)
2. Then show the **explanation** (drivers)
3. Then show the **method** (credibility)

Copy guidelines:
- simple
- assertive
- neutral
- no hype
- no “AI magic” language

---

## CONSTRAINTS

- This is a **website**, not a local application  
- Default stack: modern web (React / Next.js)  
- Use a map component suitable for polygon rendering  
- Demo data may be static or served via a lightweight API  
- Do not introduce new libraries unless clearly justified for MVP delivery  
- Do not introduce paid dependencies or services unless explicitly approved  

---

## DATA AND SECURITY  

### Data philosophy  
Urban Analytics v1 is built **entirely on open, public, or synthetic data**.  
The goal is to demonstrate **method and insight**, not data exclusivity.


## CORE DATA SOURCES (v1)

The demo uses pre-processed, open European datasets:

- **Copernicus Land Monitoring Service — High Resolution Layers**
  - Imperviousness Density (2018)
  - Tree Cover Density (2018)

  These layers are used as structural proxies for:
  - construction intensity / soil sealing
  - environmental quality and green presence

- **ISTAT — Administrative boundaries (2025)**
  Used to define the spatial units for aggregation and ranking.

All raster data is processed offline and aggregated to administrative units before being served to the web application.
No raw satellite data is processed in the browser.

---

### Copernicus-specific considerations  

Copernicus data is publicly funded, free to access, non-exclusive, regulator-trusted within the EU

However, raw Copernicus data is not served directly to the client.

For the demo:
- Copernicus-derived indicators are **pre-processed offline**
- Only **aggregated, neighborhood-level outputs** are exposed to the user.
- No raw satellite imagery or pixel-level data is displayed or downloadable

This ensures performance, clarity for non-technical users, reduced risk of misuse or misinterpretation.

---

### Security & privacy guarantees  

- No personal data is collected  
- No user accounts or authentication  
- No tracking beyond basic, anonymized analytics (if any)  
- No raw Copernicus files exposed to the browser  
- No API keys embedded in client-side code  

If a map provider requires an access token:
- it is stored as an environment variable
- treated as a secret
- never committed to version control

---

### Transparency & limitations  

The method page must clearly state:
- data sources used
- update frequency (or lack thereof)
- known limitations of satellite-derived indicators
- that Shift Score is a **signal index**, not a forecast

This transparency is a core **credibility feature**, not an afterthought.

---

## DEFINITION OF “DONE” — v1

Urban Analytics v1 is complete when:

- The website has:
  - a landing page
  - a demo page
  - a method & limitations page
- The demo page shows:
  - a city map with neighborhood polygons
  - a ranked list view
  - a neighborhood detail panel
- Clicking a neighborhood reveals:
  - its Shift Score
  - top contributing drivers
  - a short, human-readable explanation
- The method page clearly lists:
  - data sources
  - assumptions
  - limitations

---

## OPEN DECISIONS (TO RESOLVE NEXT)

- Which city to use for the demo (Rome vs another Italian city)
- Neighborhood boundary source:
  - official administrative polygons
  - or an open boundary dataset
- Shift Score approach for v1:
  - weighted, interpretable index
  - vs very simple additive model
- Which driver categories appear in the UI:
  - amenities
  - construction intensity
  - accessibility
  - environmental stress
  - mixed-use signals
- What serves as the **credibility anchor**:
  - price proxy
  - listing trend proxy
  - or a pure signal index with no outcome claim

---

## CHANGE RULE  

When scope, styling, or features drift:
- We do not argue taste
- We refer back to this Knowledge File

This document defines:
- MVP scope
- non-goals
- constraints
- definition of done

All decisions must align with it.
